skill.name: event-store-design
skill.description: Design and implement event stores for event-sourced systems. Use when building event sourcing infrastructure, choosing event store technologies, or implementing event persistence patterns.
skill.summary: Design event stores for event-sourced systems with best practices for PostgreSQL, EventStoreDB, and DynamoDB implementations.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The skill contains documentation-only content with code examples. Backtick detections are markdown code fences, not shell execution. Fetch detections are PostgreSQL asyncpg database calls, not HTTP requests. No executable code or security risks present.

content.user_title: Design Event Stores for Event-Sourced Systems
content.value_statement: Building event-sourced systems requires careful event store design decisions. This skill provides proven patterns for PostgreSQL, EventStoreDB, and DynamoDB implementations with optimistic concurrency and subscription support.
actual_capabilities: Design event store schemas for PostgreSQL, EventStoreDB, and DynamoDB
Implement optimistic concurrency control for event streams
Create subscription and checkpoint mechanisms for event processing
Provide technology comparison and selection guidance
Generate SQL schemas and Python implementation examples
Recommend indexing strategies for event query patterns
limitations: Provides design guidance and code templates only - does not deploy infrastructure
Code examples require adaptation to specific project requirements
Does not include production monitoring or alerting configurations
Technology recommendations are general - specific needs may vary

use_cases.title: Greenfield Event-Sourced Application
use_cases.description: Design a complete event store infrastructure from scratch with appropriate technology selection and schema design.
use_cases.target_user: Backend architect building a new event-sourced system

use_cases.title: Migration from CRUD to Event Sourcing
use_cases.description: Plan and implement event store patterns when migrating an existing application to event sourcing architecture.

use_cases.title: Event Store Performance Optimization
use_cases.description: Optimize existing event store implementation with proper indexing, snapshotting strategies, and subscription handling.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Event Store Schema Design
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to design an event store for an e-commerce application. Help me create a PostgreSQL schema with tables for events, snapshots, and subscription checkpoints. Include appropriate indexes for stream queries and global subscriptions.

prompt_templates.title: Technology Selection Guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare EventStoreDB, PostgreSQL, Kafka, and DynamoDB for implementing an event store. My requirements are: high write throughput, need for per-stream queries, existing AWS infrastructure, and team familiarity with Python. Recommend the best option with justification.

prompt_templates.title: Event Store Implementation with Concurrency Control
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a Python event store class using asyncpg that implements optimistic concurrency control. Include methods for appending events with version checking, reading streams from a specific version, and subscribing to all events with checkpoint management.

prompt_templates.title: Production-Ready Event Store Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a complete event store architecture for a financial services application requiring audit trails. Include: event schema with correlation/causation IDs, snapshot strategy for aggregates older than 100 events, idempotent event handlers, and projection rebuild capability. Provide both schema and implementation guidance.

output_examples.input: Design a PostgreSQL event store schema with proper indexes
output_examples.output: A complete SQL schema including an events table with UUID primary keys, stream_id and version columns for stream ordering, global_position for global subscriptions, JSONB columns for event data and metadata, and indexes for stream queries, global position lookups, event type filtering, and time-based queries.

output_examples.input: How do I implement optimistic concurrency for event appending?
output_examples.output: Implementation pattern showing expected version checking before append, transaction-protected inserts with unique constraint on stream_id and version, ConcurrencyError exception when version mismatch occurs, and client-side retry logic with updated expected version.

best_practices: Use stream IDs that include aggregate type for clear organization, such as Order-uuid or Customer-uuid
Always include correlation and causation IDs in event metadata for distributed tracing across services
Implement idempotency using unique event IDs to handle duplicate writes and enable safe retries
anti_patterns: Never update or delete events after appending - events are immutable facts that form the audit trail
Avoid storing large payloads in events - keep event data small and reference external storage for large objects
Do not skip optimistic concurrency checks - this prevents data corruption from concurrent modifications

faq.question: What is the difference between event sourcing and traditional CRUD?
faq.answer: Event sourcing stores every state change as an immutable event rather than just the current state. This provides a complete audit trail, enables temporal queries, and supports event-driven architectures. Traditional CRUD only stores the current state.

faq.question: How do I handle event schema changes over time?
faq.answer: Version events from the start using a version field. Implement upcasters to transform older event versions to the current schema when reading. Avoid breaking changes when possible - prefer adding optional fields over removing or renaming existing ones.

faq.question: When should I use snapshots in an event store?
faq.answer: Use snapshots for aggregates with many events where replaying all events becomes slow. Common thresholds are 100-1000 events per stream. Snapshots store the aggregate state at a point in time, allowing you to start replay from that snapshot instead of event one.

faq.question: How do I ensure exactly-once event processing in subscriptions?
faq.answer: Make event handlers idempotent using event IDs for deduplication. Store processed event IDs or use business-level idempotency keys. Combine checkpoint persistence with idempotent handlers to achieve exactly-once semantics even with at-least-once delivery.

faq.question: Should I use a dedicated event store or a general-purpose database?
faq.answer: Dedicated event stores like EventStoreDB offer built-in event sourcing features but add operational complexity. General-purpose databases like PostgreSQL provide flexibility and leverage existing infrastructure. Choose based on team expertise, existing stack, and specific requirements.

faq.question: How do I query events by business criteria efficiently?
faq.answer: Create read models (projections) that transform events into query-optimized structures. Use database indexes on frequently queried fields. For complex queries, consider maintaining separate read databases updated via event subscriptions rather than querying the event store directly.
